skill.name: technical-writing
skill.description: Create technical specifications, architecture documents, runbooks, API references, and developer guides with structured templates and review checklists.
skill.summary: Helps teams write clear, structured technical documentation for software systems and operations.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, environment, and weak-crypto patterns, but review found they are examples inside documentation templates. No executable scripts, prompt injection text, credential exfiltration, or hidden automation were found in the reviewed files. The skill is publishable with low risk because sample commands and API token placeholders may still need careful use in generated documents.

low_findings.title: External Command Detections Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The command-like patterns are fenced markdown, shell, Mermaid, JSON, Python, YAML, and prose examples used as documentation templates. They are not scripts or instructions for the skill to execute commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content appears inside fenced examples and templates, and the skill declares only Read, Write, Edit, Glob, and Grep tools. I found no executable script file or runtime command invocation.

low_findings.title: Network URL Detections Are Sample Endpoints
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The hardcoded URLs are sample API or GitHub endpoints inside documentation templates. They do not send data or perform network requests by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each URL is embedded in an example request or setup template. No file contains code that would make these requests automatically.

low_findings.title: Credential Reference Is A Placeholder
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The API_KEY text is a placeholder in a configuration documentation template. It does not read, collect, or transmit environment variables.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is under a template section named Environment variables. I found no process environment access, secret lookup, or network exfiltration logic.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak-crypto blocker appears to match ordinary documentation words and headings, such as description, design, and response sections. No cryptographic algorithm or implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no crypto code, imports, or commands. The flagged lines are documentation descriptions and template headings.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Detections Are Diagram And Writing Terms
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The system reconnaissance hits are in style guidance, Mermaid diagram examples, and documentation tool lists. They do not inspect the host system.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations contain documentation guidance and static diagram syntax. No command reads system identity, environment details, files, processes, or network configuration.

low_findings.title: Dangerous Combination Heuristic Is Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The combined command, network, and credential signals come from unrelated documentation examples. No semantic evidence of obfuscation, exfiltration, or chained malicious behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed files are documentation-only content, and the suspicious categories do not combine in executable logic. I found no hidden instructions, encoded payloads, or data flow from secrets to a network sink.

content.value_statement: Technical teams need documents that are accurate, clear, and easy to maintain. This skill provides templates, structure, and review guidance for specs, runbooks, API docs, and architecture notes.
actual_capabilities: Structures technical specifications with goals, non-goals, design details, rollout plans, and open questions.
Creates architecture document outlines with components, data flow, design decisions, and operations sections.
Builds runbook templates that cover dependencies, deployment, rollback, monitoring, and troubleshooting.
Drafts API documentation sections for authentication, endpoints, parameters, examples, and error responses.
Provides writing checklists for clarity, consistency, visuals, examples, and maintenance.
limitations: It does not verify the technical accuracy of source systems without user-provided context.
It does not run commands, call APIs, or inspect infrastructure by itself.
It may produce generic placeholders when project details are missing.
It does not replace review by engineers, operators, or product owners.

use_cases.title: Write A Feature Specification
use_cases.description: Create a complete feature spec with problem framing, design details, rollout steps, and open questions.

use_cases.title: Prepare An Operations Runbook
use_cases.description: Document service ownership, dependencies, alerts, common failures, and recovery steps for production support.

use_cases.title: Standardize API Documentation
use_cases.description: Turn endpoint notes into a consistent API reference with authentication, parameters, examples, and errors.

prompt_templates.title: Draft A Basic Guide
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the technical-writing skill to create a clear getting-started guide for [product or feature]. The audience is [audience]. Include prerequisites, steps, examples, and a short FAQ.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Technical Spec
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the technical-writing skill to write a technical specification for [feature]. Include background, goals, non-goals, design, alternatives, risks, rollout, rollback, and open questions.

prompt_templates.title: Build A Runbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the technical-writing skill to create an operations runbook for [service]. Include service overview, dependencies, deployment, rollback, monitoring, alerts, common issues, and escalation.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Existing Documentation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the technical-writing skill to review and rewrite this documentation for [audience]. Improve structure, remove ambiguity, add missing sections, and list unresolved technical questions.

output_examples.input: Create a technical spec outline for a new notifications feature.
output_examples.output: A structured specification with overview, goals, non-goals, system design, data model notes, delivery plan, testing strategy, rollout, rollback, and open questions.

output_examples.input: Write a runbook for a payment processing service.
output_examples.output: A runbook covering ownership, dependencies, dashboards, alert response, common incidents, recovery actions, and escalation contacts.

output_examples.input: Improve API documentation for a user search endpoint.
output_examples.output: A cleaner API reference with authentication context, endpoint purpose, parameters, example request description, response explanation, and error guidance.

best_practices: Provide the target audience, product context, and source facts before asking for a document.
Ask for explicit sections when your team has a required documentation format.
Have a domain expert review generated content before publishing operational or security guidance.
anti_patterns: Do not publish generated docs without checking technical accuracy.
Do not include real secrets, private endpoints, or customer data in prompts or examples.
Do not ask for a final document before providing enough system, audience, and ownership context.

faq.question: What types of documents can this skill help create?
faq.answer: It supports technical specs, architecture docs, runbooks, API references, user guides, release notes, and changelogs.

faq.question: Does this skill execute commands from the examples?
faq.answer: No. The command snippets are documentation examples and templates. The skill itself does not run shell commands.

faq.question: Can it write for non-technical readers?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide the audience and desired depth, and it can simplify language and reduce jargon.

faq.question: Can it validate architecture decisions?
faq.answer: It can organize tradeoffs and questions, but engineers must verify the actual design and constraints.

faq.question: Can it use my team's documentation style?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide examples, headings, tone rules, or templates, and ask it to follow that format.

faq.question: Is it suitable for security or incident runbooks?
faq.answer: Yes, but treat generated content as a draft. Security and operations owners should review every step.
